name: Barid Baran V I
title: Freedom, Democracy and the Cost of Silence
summary: My speech discussed how freedom is not permanently guaranteed in any democracy and can gradually decline when citizens become indifferent. I highlighted how lack of public questioning weakens accountability, allowing power to concentrate. Using global examples and democratic principles, I emphasized that active participation is essential to protect civil liberties.
conclusion: Freedom is not sustained by constitutional documents alone but by citizens who stay informed, ask questions, and hold power accountable. When people choose comfort over responsibility, democracy quietly weakens. Protecting freedom is therefore not just a right, but a civic duty.

name: Sunanda V K
title: The Magic of Our Brains
summary: When we compare the human brain to a computer, we often borrow the wrong language. That is where the confusion begins. We say the brain stores information and retrieves memories, just like a machine. But the brain doesn't work like a hard drive. Instead, when we recall a memory, we don't simply pull out a file, we re-experience the moment. The sights, the sounds, and the emotions come alive again. To show this, I involved my classmates in a small activity, helping them feel how vivid and personal memory truly is. Another fascinating aspect of the brain is its ability to grow and adapt. Unlike computers, the brain improves by forming new neural connections and even creating new neurons. This incredible flexibility allows us to adjust to new environments, learn continuously, and evolve with every experience. In short, the brain isn't just a machine. It is a living, ever-changing system that reshapes itself with every thought and memory.

name: Sanjay Eshwaran P
title: Presentation by Sanjay Eshwaran P
summary: Talent is not just an advantage, it is a head start hard work can't fully match. A talented person learns faster, adapts quicker, and performs effortlessly. Hard work helps, but without talent, growth has limits. A natural left-handed person writes smoothly, while a trained one still struggles. That instinct cannot be fully created through practice. Talent gives efficiency, hard work gives repetition. In competition, efficiency wins. You can train someone day and night to become a world-class singer. But without natural voice control, can they compete with a talented person? Hard work improves, but talent defines the peak.
conclusion: Talent is greater than hard work.

name: Prithviraj N
title: Does Unlimited Wealth Make Humans Evil?
summary: My seminar was about the Epstein files and how some powerful billionaires, after getting bored with their normal lives, engaged in unethical and disturbing activities. The main purpose of my speech was to create awareness among people, encourage them to take serious issues seriously, and stay alert about what is happening in society.
conclusion: Is it right to generalize rich people based on one case? Do you think all billionaires are like this?

name: Lohendra V S
title: Do We Value Memory Too Much?
summary: All of us use memory every day. It helps us learn from our experiences, remember important moments, and make better decisions. Because of this, many of us naturally see memory as a blessing. At the same time, we also understood that memory can sometimes become heavy. We often hold onto comfort and repeat pleasant experiences, which may stop us from trying new challenges. Even more, we tend to remember negative moments, especially insults, longer than the help or kindness we receive. During this session, I asked all of you to share your opinion. Out of our class, 28 of you said memory is a blessing, 8 said it is a curse, and another 8 said it depends on the situation. This clearly shows that memory is not experienced in the same way by everyone. Each person’s experiences shape how they see memory. We also reflected on how children handle memories. Children do not carry grudges or overthink past mistakes. They move forward easily, which allows them to connect freely with others. This reminds us that the real issue is not memory itself, but what we choose to keep in our minds.
conclusion: Memory is neither completely a blessing nor completely a burden, it becomes what we make of it. It helps us grow when we keep the lessons, but it can trouble us when we hold onto pain and negativity.

name: Dheeksha R
title: Expectations - Hope or Hype
summary: People's expectations can be super tricky. On one hand, we all have hopes and ideals about how things should go, but on the other, everyone's got different vibes and cultural backgrounds that shape what they expect from others. When these expectations don't meet reality, disappointment and conflict can pop up, so it's clutch to communicate clearly upfront about what we want and need. One good thing about expectations is that they can motivate us to push harder and strive for better! When we set high (but realistic) expectations, it can drive us to prepare, work smart, and achieve more. Plus, meeting expectations can be super satisfying.
conclusion: In the end, expectations can be a double-edged sword – they can motivate and inspire, but also disappoint and frustrate. The key is to set realistic expectations, communicate clearly, and stay flexible. By doing so, we can harness their power while avoiding their pitfalls.

name: Sree Gireesa Vasuki S
title: Are We Losing Critical Thinking?
summary:
"The important thing is not to stop questioning." – Albert Einstein

A very warm welcome to everyone. This quote reminds us that questioning is the foundation of critical thinking. But today, in the age of social media, we must ask ourselves — are we truly questioning, or just accepting everything we see? Social media has become a powerful source of news, opinions, and knowledge, where information is fast, short, and often driven by emotions. While critical thinking requires analysis, questioning, and independent judgment, social media encourages instant reactions — like, share, scroll — without much reflection.

Let me give you a simple example. Imagine you are scrolling through social media before your exams and you come across a post claiming, "This topic is 100% coming in the exam." Without verifying the source, you trust it and focus only on that topic. But in the actual exam, that topic doesn’t appear, leaving you stressed and unprepared. What happened here? You didn’t question or analyze — you simply accepted the information. In just one scroll, thinking was replaced by blind trust.

This highlights a major concern: the spread of misinformation, reduced attention span, and the creation of echo chambers where we only see what we already agree with. However, social media is not entirely negative — it can spread awareness and provide diverse perspectives. The real issue lies in how we use it. We are not completely losing our ability to think critically, but we are slowly weakening it. Therefore, we must make a conscious effort to question what we see, think deeply, and not believe everything we come across. Because in a world overflowing with information, the true power lies not in what we consume, but in how we think.
conclusion: In conclusion, as college students living in a world flooded with information, our greatest strength is not how fast we consume content, but how deeply we question it. Social media presents us with endless opinions, but not all of them are accurate or meaningful. If we simply scroll and accept, we risk becoming passive thinkers shaped by algorithms rather than our own reasoning. True intelligence today lies in the ability to pause, reflect, and analyze before believing or reacting. We must learn to verify sources, challenge assumptions, and form independent perspectives. Because the moment we stop questioning, we start losing control over our own thoughts. Therefore, the real takeaway is this: in a digital world designed to make us scroll endlessly, let us choose to stop, think critically, and stay intellectually independent.

name: Razeega S
title: Women Empowerment means Equal Opportunities and Equal Choices.
summary: Women empowerment means giving women and girls the power to make their own choices and decisions. This means they should get the same opportunities for education as boys and men do. In the past, women were not allowed to go to school; they were told to sit at home and take care of household chores. But now things are changing. Girls are going to school, becoming doctors, engineers, teachers, lawyers, and even leaders of their countries. This reminds me of a quote 'When you educate a girl, you not only educate the girl, you educate a family.' But still, in some places, girls do not get equal rights as men do, which is not fair. Women are very talented. They can do anything if they are given a chance. Mary Kom became a great boxer even after facing many difficulties. Kalpana Chawla became an astronaut and made our country proud.
conclusion: When we empower women, we make our society strong and better. So, let us all put our hands together to respect and support women everywhere

name: Sanjay N
title: Hello Friend — 3 Lessons From Mr. Robot
summary: This presentation draws inspiration from Elliot Alderson, the protagonist of Mr. Robot — a genius hacker who could see through every mask and every performance the world put on. The first lesson is about luck. Luck is real, but it gravitates toward those who keep showing up. Think of luck as a bubble in an ocean of opportunities. Hardwork is the boat that takes you to it. You don't wait. You row. The second lesson is about ambition. Elliot never chased being number one. He chased truth. Two people can compete for the same opportunity — the one focused on genuine growth sustains it, the one who only chased rank crumbles when someone better arrives. First place is a byproduct. Mastery is the goal. The third lesson is about judgment. There is a difference between a pattern and a moment. A single bad moment does not define a person. Everyone in this room is carrying something invisible. Extend the understanding you would want for yourself. Mr. Robot was never about hacking. It was about one person deciding his life was worth fighting for. That decision belongs to all of us.
conclusion: Mr. Robot was never really about hacking. It was about one person deciding that his life was worth fighting for despite trauma, illness, and a system stacked against him. The same choice belongs to every one of us. Stop waiting for luck. Stop chasing rank. Stop reducing people to their worst chapters. Your life is worth fighting for. Start today.

name: Sri Avinash Karthik C
title: Presentation by Sri Avinash Karthik C

name: Uthaya Krishnan G R
title: Presentation by Uthaya Krishnan G R
